heroOverview: A cystocele is a herniation of the posterior wall of the bladder through the anterior vaginal wall, creating a bulge into the vaginal canal. It is the most common form of pelvic organ prolapse, affecting an estimated 9–41% of parous women to some degree depending on the measurement criteria used. The fundamental cause is weakening of the pubocervical fascial support layer between the bladder and vagina — typically from the cumulative effects of vaginal childbirth, ageing, and oestrogen decline after menopause. Symptoms range from a sensation of vaginal pressure or bulge to incomplete bladder emptying, recurrent urinary tract infections, and urinary incontinence. Mild cases are managed conservatively with pelvic floor muscle training and a vaginal pessary; surgery is reserved for symptomatic prolapse that does not respond to conservative measures.

keyStats.prevalence: Pelvic organ prolapse affects 3–6% of women with notable symptoms; symptomatic cystocele is the most common type, with anatomical prolapse detectable in 40–50% of parous women on examination (ACOG, 2019)
keyStats.demographics: Almost exclusively affects women; risk rises sharply after vaginal delivery and after menopause
keyStats.avgAge: Symptomatic presentation peaks in the fifth to seventh decades; anatomical prolapse is detectable in younger women after childbirth
keyStats.globalCases: Pelvic organ prolapse results in approximately 300,000 surgical procedures annually in the United States alone (CDC / NHDS data)

definition: A cystocele (ICD-10: N81.10, unspecified) is a type of pelvic organ prolapse in which the bladder descends into or beyond the anterior vaginal wall due to failure of the endopelvic fascial support structures. The supporting layer — the pubocervical fascia — normally holds the bladder base elevated above the vaginal lumen; when it weakens, tears, or detaches from its lateral apical supports, the bladder rotates downward and the vaginal skin bulges. Severity is graded using the Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification (POP-Q) system: stage 1 is above the hymen, stage 2 is at the hymenal ring, stages 3 and 4 represent increasing degrees of descent beyond the hymen. The condition falls under urogynaecology and urology. It often coexists with uterine prolapse, rectocele, and pelvic floor insufficiency. The ICD-10 unspecified code N81.10 is used when the degree of cystocele cannot be further specified; N81.11 and N81.12 denote mild/moderate and severe forms respectively. Cystocele is distinct from urethrocele (urethral prolapse), though both can occur simultaneously, and from simple urinary incontinence without prolapse.

typesClassification.description: The bladder has dropped slightly but remains inside the vagina, more than 1 cm above the hymenal ring; often asymptomatic and detected on routine pelvic examination.
The bladder has descended to within 1 cm above or below the hymenal ring; patients typically report a vaginal bulge and urinary symptoms.
The bladder protrudes more than 1 cm beyond the hymen; the bulge is often visible externally and symptoms are usually significant.
The bladder is maximally descended, essentially everting the vaginal wall; associated with severe bladder emptying problems and significant functional impairment.
Caused by lateral detachment of the pubocervical fascia from the arcus tendineus; the bladder falls to one or both sides and may be corrected surgically by reattachment.
Caused by a midline tear in the pubocervical fascia itself, producing a symmetric central bulge; repair targets the fascial defect directly.

primarySymptoms: A sensation of pelvic pressure, heaviness, or something falling out of the vagina, typically worsening throughout the day and with prolonged standing or lifting.
A visible or palpable bulge at or beyond the vaginal opening, sometimes described by patients as feeling like 'sitting on a ball.'
Urinary frequency — needing to void more often than normal, because the prolapsed bladder holds less urine efficiently.
Difficulty fully emptying the bladder (urinary retention or incomplete voiding), sometimes requiring the patient to reposition or push the bulge back manually to void.
Recurrent urinary tract infections resulting from chronic residual urine, which acts as a culture medium for bacteria.
Stress urinary incontinence — leaking urine with coughing, sneezing, or exercise — though paradoxically some women with large cystoceles develop urinary retention rather than incontinence.
A weak or interrupted urinary stream that improves when the prolapse is reduced manually.
Lower back or pelvic discomfort that worsens after prolonged standing and eases when lying down.
Dyspareunia (pain during intercourse) due to anatomical distortion of the vaginal canal.
Vaginal discomfort, dryness, or irritation of the exposed vaginal epithelium in advanced prolapse.
earlyWarningSigns: A vague sense of pelvic pressure by mid-afternoon that was not there a year ago.
First episodes of stress leaking when jumping or laughing.
Noticing that it takes longer to empty the bladder fully.
Occasional feeling of something soft at the vaginal entrance after prolonged standing.
emergencySigns: Inability to urinate at all (acute urinary retention) despite persistent urge — requires same-day catheterisation.
A large prolapse that becomes ulcerated, bleeds, or cannot be manually reduced.
Signs of kidney infection — high fever, one-sided flank pain, nausea — from a chronically obstructed urinary system.
New-onset overflow incontinence with palpable bladder distension, suggesting significant urinary obstruction.

causes.description: Vaginal delivery stretches and sometimes tears the pubocervical fascia, levator ani muscles, and their nerve supply; each vaginal delivery incrementally increases prolapse risk, and instrumental deliveries and prolonged second-stage labour multiply damage substantially.

Oestrogen maintains the collagen content and tensile strength of pelvic support structures; postmenopausal loss of oestrogen thins fascial tissue and accelerates prolapse progression, explaining the sharp rise in symptomatic cases in the fifth and sixth decades.

Repeated or sustained elevation of intra-abdominal pressure from chronic cough, constipation, heavy lifting, or obesity transmits downward force onto pelvic floor structures; over years this fatigues fascial attachments and promotes descent.

causes.cause: Inherited collagen and connective tissue weakness
causes.description: Women with hypermobility syndromes, Marfan syndrome, or genetically thin collagen fibres develop prolapse at younger ages and after fewer deliveries than the general population, indicating a constitutional support deficiency.

Hysterectomy removes the uterosacral and cardinal ligament complex that suspends the vaginal vault; this alters fascial tension and increases the long-term risk of subsequent cystocele and vault prolapse.

Sarcopenia of the levator ani muscles with ageing reduces the active pelvic floor support that augments fascial support; neurological damage from ageing further impairs the coordinated contraction needed to resist intra-abdominal pressure surges.

riskFactors.description: Each vaginal delivery approximately doubles the adjusted odds of prolapse; women with three or more vaginal deliveries face 4–5 times the risk of nulliparous women in population-based studies.
Symptomatic prolapse prevalence rises from under 3% in women under 45 to over 11% in those aged 60–79; the combination of oestrogen loss and cumulative fascial fatigue drives this increase.
BMI above 30 kg/m² is associated with a 40–75% increased risk of symptomatic pelvic organ prolapse compared with healthy weight, primarily by chronically elevating intra-abdominal pressure.
Repeated Valsalva manoeuvre during defecation transmits pressure peaks to the anterior compartment, gradually displacing the bladder neck downward.
A persistent cough generates episodic intra-abdominal pressure surges of 100 mmHg or more; smokers and COPD patients with chronic cough have measurably elevated prolapse rates.
Hysterectomy increases the lifetime risk of prolapse surgery approximately threefold; vault descent and anterior prolapse are the most common surgical sequelae.
Sisters and daughters of women with prolapse have a two- to threefold elevated risk, consistent with heritable variation in connective tissue quality and fascial anatomy.
Occupations requiring repeated heavy lifting — nursing, construction, farming — are associated with higher prolapse rates in cross-sectional studies.

affectedDemographics: Parous women, particularly those with more than one vaginal delivery
Postmenopausal women aged 50–79 in whom oestrogen deficiency accelerates progression
Women with obesity or chronic conditions that raise intra-abdominal pressure
Women who have undergone prior hysterectomy
Women with connective tissue disorders including hypermobility syndrome
Women in physically demanding occupations with repeated heavy lifting
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is primarily clinical, made on the basis of symptoms and physical examination. The clinician asks about the type, onset, and severity of urinary symptoms, the presence of a visible or palpable bulge, its relationship to activity and time of day, and whether manual replacement of the prolapse relieves symptoms. Examination is performed with the patient in the lithotomy or left lateral position, first at rest and then during a Valsalva manoeuvre or cough to bring out the full extent of prolapse. The POP-Q system provides objective staging by measuring the position of defined anatomical landmarks relative to the hymen; it is the international standard for clinical trials and surgical planning. Urinalysis and urine culture are performed when recurrent UTI is part of the history, and a post-void residual urine measurement (by ultrasound or in-and-out catheterisation) quantifies bladder emptying impairment. Urodynamic studies — cystometry and uroflowmetry — are indicated before surgical repair when incontinence is a significant symptom, to detect detrusor overactivity, genuine stress incontinence, and occult stress incontinence that may be unmasked after prolapse reduction. Cystoscopy is not routine but is performed when bladder pain, haematuria, or prior mesh surgery raises concerns. MRI defecography or dynamic pelvic MRI is used in complex multicompartment prolapse when surgical planning needs detailed anatomical mapping.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Clinically stage the prolapse, identify compartments affected, and plan management; essential before any treatment decision.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Examination with a speculum and measuring probe while you are asked to strain or cough; takes about 10 minutes; causes mild discomfort but not pain.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures residual urine volume after voluntary voiding; over 100 mL is clinically significant and guides the need for catheterisation or surgical repair.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A portable ultrasound probe is placed over the lower abdomen immediately after you urinate; quick and painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies active urinary tract infection and guides antibiotic choice; essential when recurrent UTI is part of the symptom history.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You provide a mid-stream urine sample; the dipstick result is immediate, culture takes 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises urinary incontinence mechanism (stress vs. urgency vs. overflow) before surgical planning; detects occult stress incontinence masked by the prolapse.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small catheter is placed in the bladder and another in the rectum; the bladder is filled with saline while pressure is recorded; takes 30–45 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Images the levator ani, fascial integrity, and bladder neck mobility; useful for planning repair type and assessing levator avulsion injury.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A ultrasound probe is placed on the perineum or in the vagina; mildly uncomfortable, takes 15–20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides detailed anatomical mapping of multi-compartment prolapse in complex cases or planned mesh repair, showing levator plate position and defect location.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard MRI with dynamic images taken during straining; no contrast usually needed; 30–40 minutes in the scanner.

treatmentOverview: Management is determined by symptom severity, prolapse stage, the patient's desire for childbearing, overall health, and personal preference. Many women with stage 1–2 cystocele and tolerable symptoms do well with conservative management alone. Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT), guided by a pelvic floor physiotherapist and performed consistently for at least 3–6 months, reduces prolapse severity by one stage in roughly 20% of women and substantially improves symptoms of pressure and urinary frequency in most. Vaginal pessaries — ring, Gellhorn, cube, or Shaatz types — mechanically support the prolapsed bladder and provide significant symptom relief in 50–75% of users, often indefinitely if the patient tolerates the device. Topical vaginal oestrogen in postmenopausal women improves tissue quality, eases pessary insertion, and reduces erosion risk. Surgical repair (anterior colporrhaphy) is offered to women with significant symptoms who have completed childbearing and have not been helped by conservative measures. Native tissue repair has a 5-year anatomical recurrence rate of approximately 10–30%, which is why surgery is not recommended until conservative options have been genuinely tried. Mesh augmentation, where the fascial defect is reinforced with polypropylene mesh, has higher anatomical success rates but carries specific risks of mesh erosion and pain; it is used selectively in recurrent or high-risk cases following careful informed consent.

medicalTreatments.name: Pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT)
medicalTreatments.description: Structured physiotherapy-guided programme of progressive levator ani contractions performed daily; builds active support for the bladder base and reduces symptoms of prolapse and urinary leaking. Most guidelines recommend 3 sets of 8–12 contractions daily for at least 3–6 months before judging effectiveness.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces prolapse symptoms in 50–70% of women and improves POP-Q stage by at least one grade in approximately 20% at 6 months, per Cochrane systematic review 2011 and update.

medicalTreatments.name: Vaginal pessary
medicalTreatments.description: A silicone or latex ring or other shaped device inserted to mechanically elevate and support the anterior vaginal wall. Fitted by a clinician and usually changed every 3–6 months; patients can be taught self-management. Available in over 25 shapes to accommodate varying anatomy and lifestyle.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides meaningful symptom relief in approximately 50–75% of women who are successfully fitted; retention rates of 60–70% at 1 year in most series.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical vaginal oestrogen
medicalTreatments.description: Applied to the vaginal wall as cream, pessary, or ring; restores epithelial thickness, collagen quality, and pelvic floor tissue strength in postmenopausal women. Reduces pessary erosion, discomfort, and recurrent UTI. Systemic absorption is minimal and the treatment is considered safe even in breast cancer survivors per recent ACOG guidance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces prolapse-related UTI recurrence and pessary-related discomfort; modest improvement in prolapse symptoms in 30–40% of postmenopausal users as an adjunct to other management.

medicalTreatments.name: Anticholinergic or beta-3 agonist medications
medicalTreatments.description: Used when detrusor overactivity (urge incontinence) accompanies the cystocele; medications such as solifenacin or mirabegron reduce urinary urgency and frequency. They do not treat the structural prolapse but improve quality of life when urgency is a dominant symptom.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce urgency incontinence episodes by approximately 50–70% compared with placebo in overactive bladder trials when used for the urge component of mixed symptoms.

medicalTreatments.name: Clean intermittent self-catheterisation (CISC)
medicalTreatments.description: For women with significant post-void residual urine causing recurrent UTI or overflow symptoms, CISC allows complete bladder emptying without indwelling catheter, reducing infection risk while awaiting or instead of surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eliminates symptomatic residual urine in virtually 100% of patients who can perform the technique correctly; reduces UTI frequency by approximately 50% compared with non-catheterised controls in retention.

surgicalOptions.name: Anterior colporrhaphy (native tissue repair)
surgicalOptions.description: The most common surgical repair; the anterior vaginal wall is opened and the pubocervical fascia is plicated or sutured to strengthen the bladder support. Performed vaginally under regional or general anaesthesia. Recovery 4–6 weeks.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Anatomical success (POP-Q stage 0–1) in approximately 70–90% at 1 year; 5-year recurrence rates of 10–30% in most series.

surgicalOptions.name: Paravaginal defect repair
surgicalOptions.description: Targets a lateral fascial detachment by reattaching the pubocervical fascia to the arcus tendineus fasciae pelvis; can be done vaginally or laparoscopically. Preferred when lateral defect is confirmed on examination or imaging.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Anatomical cure reported in 75–95% of correctly selected cases in retrospective series; randomised trial data limited.

surgicalOptions.name: Mesh-augmented anterior repair
surgicalOptions.description: A polypropylene mesh is placed under the anterior vaginal wall to reinforce the fascial repair; used in recurrent cystocele or high-risk anatomy. Regulated strictly in many countries (including the UK and Australia) following mesh complications data.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces anatomical recurrence by 10–15% compared with native tissue repair in meta-analyses, but at the cost of 5–10% mesh erosion risk requiring further surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic paravaginal repair
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive approach using laparoscopic instruments to reattach the fascia to the arcus tendineus; offers faster recovery than open abdominal approaches with comparable success rates.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Anatomical success approximately 70–90% in published series with acceptably low complication rates.

alternativeTreatments.name: Biofeedback-assisted PFMT
alternativeTreatments.description: Uses sensor probes or external EMG to give real-time visual feedback on pelvic floor muscle contractions, improving technique and compliance with PFMT; shown to enhance outcomes compared with unsupervised exercise in systematic reviews.

alternativeTreatments.name: Electrical stimulation
alternativeTreatments.description: Mild electrical current applied via a vaginal or surface electrode stimulates pelvic floor muscle contraction in women who cannot voluntarily contract adequately; a useful adjunct in early rehabilitation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Yoga and Pilates-based core training
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured programmes incorporating deep abdominal and pelvic floor activation may support conservative management, although high-quality trial evidence specific to cystocele is limited.

whySeeSpecialist: A urogynaecologist or pelvic floor urologist should be involved when a vaginal bulge is visible, when urinary symptoms are significantly impairing daily life or causing recurrent infections, when conservative management has been tried for 3–6 months without adequate benefit, or when surgery is being considered. Specialist assessment provides accurate POP-Q staging, urodynamic evaluation if needed, and discussion of the full range of management options including different pessary types and surgical techniques.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a urogynaecologist or female pelvic medicine and reconstructive surgeon with a dedicated prolapse clinic offering on-site urodynamics and fitting of multiple pessary types. Verify that the surgeon has formal training in mesh complications management if mesh surgery is contemplated, and ask about their personal surgical volume and recurrence rates for anterior repair. A centre with access to a specialist pelvic floor physiotherapist allows conservative management to be optimised before surgical decisions are made.
preventionStrategies: Perform regular pelvic floor muscle exercises from the first trimester of pregnancy and continue postpartum, as this reduces prolapse severity after delivery.
Manage constipation with adequate fibre, fluid, and if necessary bulk laxatives — avoid habitual straining at stool.
Maintain a healthy body weight to reduce chronic intra-abdominal pressure on pelvic support structures.
Treat chronic cough aggressively; stopping smoking substantially reduces cough-related prolapse pressure.
Use topical vaginal oestrogen after the menopause to maintain fascial tissue quality.
Avoid prolonged heavy lifting in the immediate postpartum period; build core and pelvic floor strength progressively before returning to strenuous exercise.
lifestyleModifications: Do pelvic floor exercises daily — consistency over months is what builds strength; app-based or physiotherapist-guided programmes improve adherence.
Avoid lifting objects heavier than comfortable without co-activating the pelvic floor first (exhale and brace).
Elevate the foot of the bed slightly or rest for 20–30 minutes in the afternoon on days when prolapse symptoms are worse.
Reduce fluid intake slightly in the evenings if nocturia is a problem, but maintain daytime hydration to reduce UTI risk.
Wear a pessary for activities that worsen symptoms — long walks, exercise, travel — even if not used full-time.
Contact a pelvic floor physiotherapist before returning to high-impact exercise such as running after delivery.

recommended: High-fibre diet (25–35 g per day) from vegetables, legumes, whole grains, and fruit to prevent constipation and straining
Adequate hydration — 1.5–2 litres of water daily — to maintain soft stools and reduce UTI risk
Probiotic foods such as yoghurt and kefir to support healthy perineal and vaginal flora
Calcium and vitamin D for bone health, particularly important in postmenopausal women on oestrogen
avoid: Low-fibre processed foods that predispose to constipation and straining at stool
Excess caffeine and alcohol, which irritate the bladder and worsen urinary urgency and frequency
Very high fluid intake close to bedtime if nocturia is disruptive
Weight gain through calorie-dense diets, which adds chronic intra-abdominal pressure

exerciseGuidelines: Pelvic floor muscle training should be performed daily and is the cornerstone of exercise for cystocele management. Low-impact activities — walking, swimming, cycling — are well tolerated and recommended. High-impact activities such as running, jumping, and heavy resistance training increase intra-abdominal pressure and can worsen symptoms; they can be resumed gradually once pelvic floor strength has been built up, ideally with guidance from a pelvic floor physiotherapist who can assess impact during exercise. Avoid breath-holding (Valsalva) during lifting; exhale with effort and engage the pelvic floor before loading.
dailyManagement: Insert the pessary each morning and remove it before bed, or manage on the schedule agreed with the clinician.
Apply topical oestrogen cream as prescribed — consistent use prevents erosion and maintains tissue health.
Complete a 3-day bladder diary periodically to track frequency, volume, and incontinence episodes for clinic review.
Perform pelvic floor exercises at the same time each day — link them to a daily habit such as teeth brushing to improve adherence.
Report new-onset lower back pain, fever, or haematuria to the clinician promptly, as these may indicate UTI or obstructive uropathy.
Return for pessary care every 3–6 months, or sooner if you notice discharge, odour, or difficulty removing the device.
prognosis: Cystocele rarely resolves spontaneously after it has become symptomatic, but the majority of women achieve good quality of life with appropriate management. Conservative measures — pelvic floor training and pessary — achieve meaningful symptom control in 50–75% of women who use them consistently. Prolapse is rarely life-threatening, but long-standing stage 3–4 cystocele causing significant urinary retention can lead to upper urinary tract damage in a small proportion of cases. Native tissue surgical repair provides good anatomical and symptomatic results in roughly 70–90% of patients in the medium term, with recurrence rates of 10–30% at 5 years. Recurrent prolapse after repair carries a somewhat higher surgical complication risk, which reinforces the value of optimising conservative treatment first.
recoveryTimeline: After anterior colporrhaphy, most women are discharged the same day or after one night in hospital. Pelvic rest (no intercourse, no heavy lifting over 5 kg, no high-impact activity) is advised for 6–8 weeks while fascial healing occurs. Most women return to light daily activities within 1–2 weeks and to full activities by 8–12 weeks. A pelvic floor physiotherapy programme is recommended from 6 weeks post-surgery to prevent recurrence. Full surgical healing and final assessment of anatomical outcome typically take 3–6 months.
complications: Recurrent urinary tract infections from chronic post-void residual urine — affects a significant proportion of women with stage 3–4 cystocele.
Urinary retention and overflow incontinence in advanced cases, sometimes requiring intermittent self-catheterisation.
Occult stress incontinence unmasked after surgical prolapse reduction — urodynamic pre-operative testing reduces surgical surprise.
Mesh erosion into the vagina or bladder, causing discharge, pain, and the need for further surgery — a recognised complication of mesh-augmented repairs.
Sexual dysfunction, including dyspareunia and reduced sexual satisfaction, from anatomical distortion and psychological impact of prolapse.
Upper urinary tract obstruction with hydronephrosis and renal impairment in severe, long-standing untreated cystocele — rare but serious.

supportResources.name: ACOG — Pelvic Organ Prolapse
supportResources.description: Patient FAQ from the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists covering causes, symptoms, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: NICE — Pelvic Organ Prolapse in Women
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline on diagnosis, non-surgical and surgical management of pelvic organ prolapse including cystocele.

supportResources.name: Bladder and Bowel Community UK
supportResources.description: Patient information and peer support resources for women with bladder prolapse and pelvic floor dysfunction.

sources.title: NICE NG229 — Pelvic organ prolapse in women: management (2023)
Maher C et al. Surgical management of pelvic organ prolapse in women. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2018
Hagen S et al. Individualised pelvic floor muscle training in women with pelvic organ prolapse (POPPY trial). Lancet, 2014
ACOG Practice Bulletin 214 — Pelvic Organ Prolapse (2019)
Dumoulin C et al. Pelvic floor muscle training versus no treatment, or inactive control treatments, for urinary incontinence in women. Cochrane Database, 2018

Haylen BT et al. An International Urogynecological Association/International Continence Society joint terminology and classification of the complications related directly to the insertion of prostheses (meshes, implants, tapes) in female pelvic floor surgery. IUGA/ICS 2017
Wu JM et al. Prevalence and trends of symptomatic pelvic floor disorders in U.S. women. Obstetrics and Gynecology, 2014

confusedWithConditions.name: Uterine prolapse
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Uterine prolapse involves descent of the uterus and cervix into or through the vagina; the two conditions frequently coexist but are anatomically distinct and require specific repair strategies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Interstitial cystitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Interstitial cystitis causes chronic pelvic pain and urinary urgency with negative cultures but no vaginal bulge, no descensus on examination, and no improvement with pessary use.

confusedWithConditions.name: Overactive bladder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Overactive bladder produces urgency and frequency due to detrusor muscle hyperactivity, without any structural prolapse visible on pelvic examination; these conditions can coexist, complicating diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cervical stump prolapse
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cervical stump prolapse occurs only after subtotal hysterectomy and presents as prolapse of the remaining cervix into the vaginal canal, without a distinct anterior bladder bulge.

relatedConditions.name: Urinary tract infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic residual urine from bladder emptying impairment in cystocele predisposes to recurrent bacterial UTI.

relatedConditions.name: Uterine prolapse
relatedConditions.relevance: Uterine prolapse frequently accompanies cystocele and may require simultaneous repair at the same surgical procedure.

relatedConditions.name: Overactive bladder
relatedConditions.relevance: Detrusor overactivity is a common co-diagnosis in women with cystocele, and distinguishing the two determines the appropriate treatment approach.

relatedConditions.name: Interstitial cystitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Pelvic pain and urinary urgency in women with prolapse need to be distinguished from interstitial cystitis, which requires different management.

relatedConditions.name: Cystitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Recurrent cystitis is a direct consequence of the post-void residual urine that accumulates in the prolapsed bladder base.

relatedConditions.name: Kidney stones
relatedConditions.relevance: Urinary stasis from chronic incomplete emptying raises the risk of bladder and ureteral calculi in women with advanced cystocele.

faqs.question: What is a cystocele?
faqs.answer: A cystocele is a bladder prolapse where the wall between the bladder and vagina weakens, allowing the bladder to bulge into the vaginal canal. It is the most common form of pelvic organ prolapse and causes symptoms such as vaginal pressure, urinary frequency, and difficulty fully emptying the bladder.

faqs.question: Can a cystocele heal on its own?
faqs.answer: A cystocele does not heal spontaneously once it has become symptomatic, but it does not always worsen either. Consistent pelvic floor exercises and weight management can prevent progression and significantly reduce symptoms in many women, particularly at milder stages. Prolapse rarely reverses without treatment.

faqs.question: How is a cystocele treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: Non-surgical options include supervised pelvic floor muscle training performed daily for at least 3–6 months, a vaginal pessary fitted by a clinician to mechanically support the bladder, topical vaginal oestrogen in postmenopausal women to improve tissue quality, and lifestyle changes such as weight loss and constipation management.

faqs.question: Does a cystocele cause urinary incontinence?
faqs.answer: A cystocele can cause stress urinary incontinence from altered urethral support, but paradoxically large cystoceles sometimes cause urinary retention and difficulty emptying rather than leaking. Both mechanisms can coexist. Urodynamic testing before surgery is important to identify which problem is dominant.

faqs.question: Is cystocele surgery safe?
faqs.answer: Anterior colporrhaphy — the standard native tissue repair — is a well-established procedure with low serious complication rates. The main concerns are recurrence (10–30% at 5 years), de novo stress incontinence, and, if mesh is used, erosion risk. Most women return to normal activities within 6–8 weeks. The decision to operate should follow a thorough discussion of risks and benefits.

faqs.question: What exercises help a cystocele?
faqs.answer: Pelvic floor muscle exercises (Kegel exercises) are the single most effective exercise. Aim for 3 sets of 10 sustained contractions (holding for 5–10 seconds) daily. Low-impact activity such as walking and swimming is encouraged. Avoid high-impact exercise and heavy lifting until pelvic floor strength has improved, ideally assessed by a physiotherapist.

faqs.question: Can I use a pessary long-term?
faqs.answer: Yes. Many women use a vaginal pessary as their long-term management for years or decades, particularly those who are not surgical candidates or prefer to avoid surgery. The pessary needs to be cleaned and refitted every 3–6 months by a clinician, and topical oestrogen is usually added to prevent tissue erosion.

faqs.question: What causes a cystocele after childbirth?
faqs.answer: Vaginal delivery stretches and can tear the pubocervical fascia that supports the bladder. The levator ani muscles and their nerve supply are also damaged during delivery, particularly in prolonged or instrumental births. These injuries may not produce symptoms immediately but can become apparent years later as oestrogen declines with the menopause.

faqs.question: Can a cystocele affect the kidneys?
faqs.answer: In advanced, long-standing cystocele causing significant urinary retention, urine can back up through the ureters and gradually dilate the upper urinary tract (hydronephrosis). This is uncommon but can impair kidney function over time. Women with large cystoceles and elevated post-void residual urine should have kidney function monitored.

faqs.question: Does cystocele cause pain?
faqs.answer: The most common sensation is pressure, heaviness, or dragging in the pelvis rather than sharp pain. Some women experience lower back discomfort or dyspareunia. Actual pain is less typical and if present should prompt investigation for other causes such as cystitis, endometriosis, or interstitial cystitis.

faqs.question: Can I still have sex with a cystocele?
faqs.answer: Intercourse is safe with a cystocele and is not contraindicated. Some women find it uncomfortable if the prolapse is advanced; removing the pessary before intercourse is recommended. After surgery, pelvic rest for 6–8 weeks is advised to allow healing. Many women report improved sexual satisfaction after successful repair.

faqs.question: What does a cystocele feel like?
faqs.answer: Most women describe a sensation of pelvic heaviness or pressure that worsens during the day and with prolonged standing or activity, and eases when lying down. Some notice a soft bulge at the vaginal entrance, feel that they cannot empty their bladder fully, or experience recurrent bladder infections.

faqs.question: Will a cystocele get worse with age?
faqs.answer: Cystocele tends to progress over time, particularly around menopause when oestrogen decline reduces fascial tissue quality. However, progression is not inevitable — consistent pelvic floor training, weight management, and topical oestrogen can slow or stabilise mild to moderate prolapse for years.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with a cystocele?
faqs.answer: Low-impact exercise — walking, swimming, cycling, yoga — is safe and recommended. High-impact exercise such as running, jumping, and heavy weightlifting can worsen symptoms by increasing intra-abdominal pressure. A pelvic floor physiotherapist can assess which activities are appropriate and guide progressive return to exercise.

faqs.question: What is the recovery time after cystocele repair surgery?
faqs.answer: Most women are discharged within 24 hours. Pelvic rest — no intercourse, no heavy lifting, no strenuous exercise — is advised for 6–8 weeks. Light activities can resume within 1–2 weeks. Most women return to full activities by 8–12 weeks. Starting pelvic floor physiotherapy from 6 weeks post-surgery helps prevent recurrence.

faqs.question: Is cystocele more common after menopause?
faqs.answer: Yes. While structural damage from childbirth may occur years earlier, symptoms often emerge or worsen at menopause when falling oestrogen reduces collagen and tissue strength. The combination of prior fascial injury and oestrogen loss explains why symptomatic cystocele peaks in the 50s and 60s.

faqs.question: Can I prevent a cystocele from recurring after surgery?
faqs.answer: Long-term pelvic floor exercise after surgery significantly reduces recurrence risk. Managing constipation, maintaining a healthy weight, avoiding heavy lifting without proper technique, using topical oestrogen if postmenopausal, and treating any chronic cough all help protect the repair.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a cystocele and a rectocele?
faqs.answer: A cystocele is a prolapse of the bladder into the front (anterior) wall of the vagina, causing urinary symptoms. A rectocele is a prolapse of the rectum into the back (posterior) wall of the vagina, causing symptoms of difficult bowel emptying and the need to splint the vaginal wall to defecate. Both can coexist.

faqs.question: Can a cystocele cause recurrent UTIs?
faqs.answer: Yes. When the bladder does not empty completely due to the prolapse, residual urine stagnates and provides an environment for bacterial growth. Women with cystocele have higher rates of recurrent UTI than the general population, and treating the prolapse — either with a pessary or surgery — often substantially reduces UTI frequency.

faqs.question: Do I need surgery for a cystocele?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Surgery is reserved for women with significant symptoms after at least 3–6 months of conservative treatment with pelvic floor training and pessary trial. Many women manage well without surgery for years. Surgery is appropriate when symptoms impair quality of life and conservative options have been genuinely optimised.

metaDescription: A cystocele (bladder prolapse) causes a vaginal bulge, pelvic pressure and urinary problems. Learn causes, stages, treatment and when surgery helps.
